title: Apply: America's Experiment in Interracial Democracy
description: Apply your understanding of how the United States has at times denied and allowed Black people to vote in elections with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Apply: America's Experiment in Interracial Democracy

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative George Boutwell’s comments in 1869 on the Proposed Suffrage Bill and Suffrage Amendment, also known as the Fifteenth Amendment.**

[T]here is no provision in the Constitution by which the United States is denied the power of [[☃ definition 1]] the right of citizens to vote. There is, in the fourteenth article of amendments to the Constitution, a limitation upon the power of the States in that respect, but none upon the power of the United States. The amendment which we propose secures the people against any abridgement of their electoral power, either by the United States or by the States. In that alone there is sufficient reason to justify the amendment.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Any new amendments to protect the right to vote would not be effective, as states have the power to regulate elections.
The Fourteenth Amendment already secures everyone’s right to vote, and the right to vote is an important right of all citizens.
Any measures to give Black men the right to vote will fail because the southern states will not agree to them.
A new constitutional amendment would protect people’s right to vote from being taken away by either the federal government or the states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative George Boutwell’s comments in 1869 on the Proposed Suffrage Bill and Suffrage Amendment, also known as the Fifteenth Amendment.**

[T]here is no provision in the Constitution by which the United States is denied the power of [[☃ definition 1]] the right of citizens to vote. There is, in the fourteenth article of amendments to the Constitution, a limitation upon the power of the States in that respect, but none upon the power of the United States. The amendment which we propose secures the people against any abridgement of their electoral power, either by the United States or by the States. In that alone there is sufficient reason to justify the amendment.

**Which statement best describes why Boutwell believes the Fifteenth Amendment is necessary?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There are no provisions in the Constitution defining citizenship.
There is no explicit federal protection for a person’s right to vote.
There is an increase in intimidation and violence against Black voters.
There should be more limitations on who has the right to vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Enforcement Bill and the Repassage of the 1866 Civil Rights Act (1870).**

That if by or under the authority of the constitution or laws of any State, or the laws of any Territory, any act is or shall be required to be done as a [[☃ definition 1]] or qualification for voting, and by such constitution or laws persons or officers are or shall be charged with the performance of duties in furnishing to citizens an opportunity to perform such prerequisite, and to become qualified to vote without distinction of race, color, or previous condition of servitude; and if any such person or officer shall refuse or knowingly omit to give full effect to this section, he shall, for every offense, forfeit and pay the sum of five hundred dollars to the person [[☃ definition 2]] thereby . . . be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor . . . or be imprisoned not less than one month, and not more than one year. 

**Which statement best summarizes the purpose of this law?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: something that must be done in advance
harmed by a denial of one's rights

choices.content: To extend citizenship rights to formerly enslaved persons
To elect Black representatives to state and federal legislatures
To eliminate obstacles to voting for Black Americans
To legalize military occupation of the southern states

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Enforcement Bill and the Repassage of the 1866 Civil Rights Act (1870).**

That if by or under the authority of the constitution or laws of any State, or the laws of any Territory, any act is or shall be required to be done as a [[☃ definition 1]] or qualification for voting, and by such constitution or laws persons or officers are or shall be charged with the performance of duties in furnishing to citizens an opportunity to perform such prerequisite, and to become qualified to vote without distinction of race, color, or previous condition of servitude; and if any such person or officer shall refuse or knowingly omit to give full effect to this section, he shall, for every offense, forfeit and pay the sum of five hundred dollars to the person [[☃ definition 2]] thereby . . . be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor . . . or be imprisoned not less than one month, and not more than one year. 

**What was the effect of legislation like the law described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: something that must be done in advance
harmed by a denial of one's rights

choices.content: There was a positive effect, as laws like this gave the national government more power to protect the voting rights of Black Americans.
There was a neutral effect, as this legislation was effective only in the southern states, not in the North.
There was a negative effect, as laws like this allowed southern states to impose more restrictions on Black voters.
There was no effect, as Black voters rarely faced obstacles to voting and the law was unnecessary.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1870 memo sent from Governor Robert K. Scott of South Carolina to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

For four years the National Government conducted a war for [[☃ definition 1]] of the Union and the establishment and preservation of the Liberty of the people[,] and those who were its opponents throughout that desperate struggle are the same class of men who now resist its policy and defy the [[☃ definition 2]] authorities, in their efforts to conform the State Government to the new order things. In the struggle for National existence the United States Government made citizens of four millions of human beings, who had previously been kept in ignorance and poverty, and thus assumed the responsibility of their education and protection; and if the state is powerless to secure these people their natural rights[,] the duty clearly [[☃ definition 3]] protection and the shield of its authority.

**Which statement best summarizes the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the continuation of something; the act of continuing to exist
is delegated or passed on to

choices.content: The federal government needs to protect Black citizens by stopping people who resist the government's efforts to change state laws.
The federal government needs to extend the right to vote to Black men and women, as they do not have the power to exercise their rights right now.
The federal government has not gone far enough to change state policies, so Black Americans risk being enslaved again.
The federal government has ushered in a new era, where a majority of Black people are exercising their right to vote for the first time.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1870 memo sent from Governor Robert K. Scott of South Carolina to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

For four years the National Government conducted a war for [[☃ definition 1]] of the Union and the establishment and preservation of the Liberty of the people[,] and those who were its opponents throughout that desperate struggle are the same class of men who now resist its policy and defy the [[☃ definition 2]] authorities, in their efforts to conform the State Government to the new order things. In the struggle for National existence the United States Government made citizens of four millions of human beings, who had previously been kept in ignorance and poverty, and thus assumed the responsibility of their education and protection; and if the state is powerless to secure these people their natural rights[,] the duty clearly [[☃ definition 3]] protection and the shield of its authority.

**This memo is evidence of which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the continuation of something; the act of continuing to exist
is delegated or passed on to

choices.content: An effort by the federal government to limit the rights of Black voters
An increase in violence against Black Americans trying to vote in the South
An effort by suffragists to secure women’s voting rights at the national level
An increase in the number of Black representatives in Congress and state legislatures

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “Reconstruction Nationalized,” an article written by Louis Jennings and published in *The New York Times* in 1870.**

The Fifteenth Amendment therefore became necessary, not only to harmonize the conditions of suffrage throughout the Union with the conditions imposed upon the South, but to [[☃ definition 1]] those [people in] the South who had cooperated with the National Government against possible political [[☃ definition 2]] in their own States. 

The Amendment does this without [[☃ definition 3]] the control of States over the question of suffrage. It neither enacts universal suffrage, nor forbids the applications of tests, whether of education or property, as qualifications for voters. It simply forbids unjust discrimination in the enforcement of tests. . . . The measure was the completion of the work of which emancipation was the [[☃ definition 4]]. It purges the Union of the last taint of slavery, and makes Reconstruction national.

**According to the excerpt, how did the Fifteenth Amendment affect states’ control over elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: States no longer had the power to regulate who could vote in elections, as that power belonged to the federal government.
States retained all powers over setting qualifications for voting, including denying people based on race.
States had to submit any proposed qualifications for voting to the federal government to determine that they did not target Black voters.
States could still determine qualifications for voting, but could not use racial qualifications.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “Reconstruction Nationalized,” an article written by Louis Jennings and published in *The New York Times* in 1870.**

The Fifteenth Amendment therefore became necessary, not only to harmonize the conditions of suffrage throughout the Union with the conditions imposed upon the South, but to [[☃ definition 1]] those [people in] the South who had cooperated with the National Government against possible political [[☃ definition 2]] in their own States. 

The Amendment does this without [[☃ definition 3]] the control of States over the question of suffrage. It neither enacts universal suffrage, nor forbids the applications of tests, whether of education or property, as qualifications for voters. It simply forbids unjust discrimination in the enforcement of tests. . . . The measure was the completion of the work of which emancipation was the [[☃ definition 4]]. It purges the Union of the last taint of slavery, and makes Reconstruction national.

**According to Jennings, what primary problem did the Fifteenth Amendment seek to correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The continuation of slavery in the border states following the federal order for emancipation
The unclear requirements for southern states to rejoin the Union after the Civil War
The fact that Black Americans were citizens under federal law but were often denied voting rights in the South
The use of barriers to voting like literacy tests or poll taxes in southern states but not in northern states

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: “The color line still exists—in this case” by Thomas Nast, *Harper’s Weekly*, 1979. Image provided by the [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2002710390/)) is in the public domain.

options.alt: Political cartoon showing Mr. Solid South writing on wall, "Education Qualification. The Black man ought to be educated before he can vote with us Whites, signed Mr. Solid South.” Most of the words on the wall are misspelled.
options.caption: “The color line still exists—in this case” by Thomas Nast, 1879.

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment extended the right to vote to Black men, giving them more political power.
Black men faced intimidation at polling places, forcing them to vote for Democratic candidates.
The Fifteenth Amendment granted citizenship to Black men, but Northern states still denied their right to vote.
White men did not have to pass literacy tests to vote in elections, as the tests were a means to disenfranchise Black voters.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**What is Nast criticizing in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: “The color line still exists—in this case” by Thomas Nast, *Harper’s Weekly*, 1979. Image provided by the [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2002710390/)) is in the public domain.

options.alt: Political cartoon showing Mr. Solid South writing on wall, "Education Qualification. The Black man ought to be educated before he can vote with us Whites, signed Mr. Solid South.” Most of the words on the wall are misspelled.
options.caption: “The color line still exists—in this case” by Thomas Nast, 1879.

choices.content: The requirement that Black voters pass literacy tests
The requirement that Black voters provide proof of a college education
The requirement that Black voters pay poll taxes
The requirement that Black voters prove their identity

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an October 1870 memo sent from Robert K. Scott, the first governor of the reconstructed South Carolina, to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

Reliable information has reached this Department that an organized force has appeared at Laurens Court House [in South Carolina], assailed the [[☃ definition 1]] driving them from their position, and killing and wounding several of them, together with a number of private citizens. These [[☃ definition 2]] seized upon the arms on deposit belonging to the State and drove many peaceful inhabitants from their homes creating a general reign of terror and lawlessness. Colored men and women have been dragged from their homes at the dead hour of night and most cruelly and brutally [[☃ definition 3]] for the sole reason that they dared to exercise their own opinions upon political subjects.

**This memo is an example of which development during Reconstruction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The formation of the Confederate States of America after southern secession
The rise in violence against Black Americans to suppress their right to vote
The spread of protests against the government for ending slavery
The emergence of the Civil Rights Movement to secure civil rights for Black people

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an October 1870 memo sent from Robert K. Scott, the first governor of the reconstructed South Carolina, to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

Reliable information has reached this Department that an organized force has appeared at Laurens Court House [in South Carolina], assailed the [[☃ definition 1]] driving them from their position, and killing and wounding several of them, together with a number of private citizens. These [[☃ definition 2]] seized upon the arms on deposit belonging to the State and drove many peaceful inhabitants from their homes creating a general reign of terror and lawlessness. Colored men and women have been dragged from their homes at the dead hour of night and most cruelly and brutally [[☃ definition 3]] for the sole reason that they dared to exercise their own opinions upon political subjects.

**The violence described in the excerpt was a response to what development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The secession of southern states
The assassination of Abraham Lincoln
The extension of suffrage to Black men
The end of Reconstruction efforts in the South
